results.possibleIdentity: Lorna Shanks was a graphic design researcher, writer, and advertising professional active in the New York design and typography industry during the 1970s and 1980s. She is best known for her long-standing association with the International Typeface Corporation (ITC), where she served in various capacities including Research Director, Advertising Manager, and Contributing Editor for the influential graphic design magazine U&lc (Upper and Lower Case). Her work often focused on the intersection of design, technology, and communications; for example, in 1977, she designed and wrote an article on the 'AlphaComp' typesetting system for an ITC publication. In 1975, she was a participant in the Second Federal Design Assembly, where she joined a panel discussion titled 'Does It Have to Be Printed?' alongside other design figures like Eli Cantor and Harley Parker, which was later published in Design Quarterly (No. 94/95). Additionally, she is listed in National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) annual reports from 1974 and 1975, appearing in the context of the Architecture + Environmental Arts program. While her direct membership role in Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is not explicitly detailed in standard founding lists, her professional activity within the New York interdisciplinary design community and her involvement with federal design assemblies and technological research align with the E.A.T. network's goals of promoting collaborations between art, design, and science.

results.possibleIdentity: "Otto Tambellini" appears to be a name used to refer to the collaborative work of artists Otto Piene (1928–2014) and Aldo Tambellini (1930–2020), or a conflation of the two names in historical records. They were prominent figures in the art-and-technology movement of the late 1960s and early 1970s. Their most significant collaboration was "Black Gate Cologne" (1968), created for the WDR (Westdeutscher Rundfunk) television station in Germany. This production is frequently cited as the first television program ever designed by artists and involved a live intermedia event with films, light objects, and audience participation. Both artists were also featured in the landmark 1969 broadcast "The Medium Is the Medium," which showcased early video art experiments. Aldo Tambellini was a pioneer of experimental cinema and video art, known for his focus on the concept of "Black," while Otto Piene was a co-founder of the Zero group and a leader in kinetic and light art. Their work aligned with the interdisciplinary goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), though they are primarily recognized for their individual and joint contributions to video art and light sculpture.
